content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

September 15, 2009

Mr. Jesse Mainardi
The Sutton Law Firm
150 Post Street, Suite 405
San Francisco, California 94108

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. 1-09-184

Dear Mr. Mainardi:

We are in receipt of your letter requesting confirmation of telephone advice concerning the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ The Commission does not provide written confirmation of telephone advice; however, we can respond to your questions concerning the campaign reporting provisions of the Act.² Because your letter does not request advice with regard to a specific committee or set of facts, we have treated your request as one for informal assistance.³

FACTS

Your questions concern a current officeholder in the City and County of San Francisco who controls a statewide primarily-formed ballot measure committee. The officeholder is not a candidate in any jurisdiction outside of San Francisco. The officeholder generally files with the San Francisco Ethics Commission because he is a

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All statutory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Telephone advice by its nature is informal and is based on information and research that must be gathered in a short period of time. For this reason, we do not confirm telephone advice by way of a written reply. However, we will provide written advice with respect to prospective actions when it is specifically requested. With this process, there is no guarantee that the written advice will match the prior informal assistance.

³ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

File No. I-09-184
Page No. 2

local candidate. The officeholder has established a state primarily formed ballot measure
committee and therefore has questions about where that ballot measure committee will
file under Regulation 18405. The state primarily formed ballot measure committee is not
yet required to file its campaign reports electronically with the Secretary of State because
it has not met the $50,000 threshold of financial activity.

DISCUSSION

Regulation 18405(a) requires a candidate who is on the ballot for election to file
preelection statements for all his or her controlled committees on the preelection filing
dates preceding his or her election. Regulation 18405 was adopted by the Commission
on May 21, 2009 and became effective July 9, 2009.

Regulation 18405(a) provides as follows:

"(a) Multiple Controlled Committees.
"(1) If a candidate or elected officer controls more than one
committee for the purpose of election to office, including committees
established for different terms of the same elective office or for a different
elective office, the candidate or elected officer shall, in addition to any
other requirements related to the filing of campaign statements, file
campaign statements for each of these committees on the dates the
candidate or elected officer is required to file preelection statements under
Sections 84200.5, 84200.7 and 84200.8 in connection with his or her
election to office. Further, a candidate or elected officer shall, in addition
to any other requirements related to the filing of campaign statements, file
campaign statements for any other committee he or she controls, including
an officeholder account committee, a legal defense fund committee, or a
ballot measure committee, on the dates the candidate or elected officer is
required to file the preelection statements under Sections 84200.5, 84200.7
and 84200.8 in connection with his or her election to office."

QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS

1. It is your understanding that the state primarily formed ballot measure
committee must file preelection statements whenever the controlling officeholder is
required to file preelection statements, but is the officeholder's campaign committee
required to file preelection statements in connection with the state primarily formed
ballot measure committee's schedule if the officeholder himself is not on the ballot?

Under Section 84215 and Regulation 18405, the local officeholder's state
primarily formed ballot measure committee is required to file preelection statements with
the local filing officer, here the San Francisco Ethics Commission, whenever the local
officeholder is on the ballot for election and is required to file preelection statements.

File No. I-09-184
Page No. 3

Under Regulation 18405(a) and prior Commission advice, however, the local
officeholder's candidate election committee is not required to file preelection statements
in connection with the state primarily formed ballot measure committee's filing schedule
for an election in which the ballot measure is being voted on but the officeholder is not.
(See Campaign Manual 3, p. 6-4, 2007 and Campaign Manual 2, p. 5-4 to 5-5, 2007.)

2. When the state primarily formed ballot measure committee must file pre-
election statements based on the controlling officeholder's filing schedule (as described
above), must the committee file a copy or an original of those statements with the
controlling officeholder's filing officer (i.e., the San Francisco Ethics Commission)?

The state primarily formed ballot measure committee files the signed originals of
its campaign statements with the Secretary of State. Under the cross filing requirements
of Regulation 18405(a), it is sufficient that the ballot measure committee file a copy, not
a signed original, of its campaign statements with the controlling officeholder's filing
officer (i.e., the San Francisco Ethics Commission).

3. Is the state primarily formed ballot measure committee required to file its
semi-annual or quarterly reports with the San Francisco Ethics Commission?

Under Regulation 18405(a), the local officeholder's state primarily formed ballot
measure committee is required to file campaign reports with the San Francisco Ethics
Commission on the preelection reporting dates for an election when the local officeholder
is on the ballot for local office. Under the regulation, however, the state primarily formed
ballot measure committee is not required to file its semi-annual or quarterly reports with
the San Francisco Ethics Commission.

Section 84215 of the Act states where campaign statements shall be filed.
Relevant to the statewide primary formed ballot measure committee, it provides:

"All candidates and elected officers and their controlled committees,
except as provided in subdivision (e), shall file one copy of the campaign
statements required by Section 84200 with the elections official of the
county in which the candidate or elected official is domiciled, as defined
in subdivision (b) of Section 349 of the Elections Code. In addition,
campaign statements shall be filed at the following places:
(a) Statewide elected officers and candidates for these offices other
than the Board of Equalization, supreme court justices, their controlled
committees, committees formed or existing primarily to support or oppose
these candidates, elected officers, supreme court justices, or statewide
measures, or the qualification of state ballot measures, and all state general
purpose committees and filers not specified in subdivisions (b) to (e),
inclusive:
(1) The original and one copy with the Secretary of State.

File No. I-09-184
Page No. 4

(2) One copy with the Registrar-Recorder of Los Angeles County.

(3) One copy with the Registrar of Voters of the City and County of
San Francisco."

Legislation that is currently pending on the floor of the State Senate, AB 1181,
sponsored by Assembly Member Huber, may change the filing requirements of Section
84215. The bill would lower the electronic filing threshold from $50,000 to $25,000, and
would eliminate the requirement for a state committee to send a copy of its campaign
statement to the Registrar-Recorder of Los Angeles County and the Department of
Elections in San Francisco.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

By: Hyla P. Wagner
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

HPW:jgl
